original_abstract:
Using high-resolution mass spectrometry, we investigated the synthesis of isoquinoline within charged electrospray droplets and the noncovalent complexation of cytochrome c with maltose during droplet fusion. Our goal was to determine if microdroplets can drive chemical transformations—both covalent and noncovalent—at higher velocities than those achievable in bulk solution. In both experimental systems, we observed dramatic rate shifts: acceleration by a factor of over one million for isoquinoline synthesis and over one thousand for protein–ligand binding. These results suggest that performing reactions in microdroplets (approximately 1–15 μm in diameter) is a robust, general method for increasing reaction rates. 

While the exact mechanism remains under investigation, droplet confinement and reagent concentration appear to be critical drivers. Notably, in our water droplet fusion experiments, high-speed imaging revealed that evaporation is negligible during the flight time before the droplets enter the mass spectrometer. This implies that evaporation is not the primary cause of acceleration in aqueous systems; instead, the unique environment of the droplet–air interface likely plays a decisive role. We propose that "microdroplet chemistry" serves as a powerful alternative for overcoming the barriers of slow or difficult reactions. When paired with mass spectrometry, this approach offers a new frontier for exploring chemical and biochemical dynamics within highly confined environments.
humanized_abstract:
We used high mass resolution mass spectrometry to study the effects of charged electrospray droplets on the reactions of isoquinoline synthesis and noncovalent binding between cytochrome c and maltose in droplet fusions. Our aim was to establish whether and how rapidly microdroplets can catalyze covalent and noncovalent chemistry in their interior and during droplet fusion. By this means, we sought to establish whether these reactions proceed with greater accelerations than those achievable in bulk solution. Rates were accelerated by a factor of more than one million for the synthesis of the heterocycle isoquinoline and by a factor of more than one thousand for the noncovalent protein-ligand binding between cytochrome c and maltose. Our results therefore demonstrate the utility of microdroplets in catalyzing reactions within the interior of the microdroplets and during their rapid fusion. Droplets of diameters ranging from 1–15 μm are therefore demonstrated to catalyze several orders of magnitude increase in reaction rates.

We do not yet know the full mechanism but confinement of droplets and concentration of reactants seem to be important. In our water droplet fusion experiments we find that evaporation is limited to such an extent that it can be ignored during the flight time prior to entry into the mass spectrometer, so it is unlikely to be the cause of the acceleration of aqueous mixtures that arise from the droplet–air interface. We propose microdroplet chemistry as an alternative means to compensate for the inherently slow nature of reactions or those that occur very slowly. When coupled with mass spectrometry this method of studying the chemistry and biochemistry under confined conditions presents considerable scope.

document.output:
